sentences.text: OK.
So I was going to school to-- in London.
It was a period when doodlebugs were starting.
Those are the V-1 flying bombs.
So this is a period when we lay in bed at night time and watch these bombs coming over and hoping that when the engine stops that you're not going to be in the target area.
But you get used to that, and--
I was living in the Griggs home.
Mr.
Griggs, who was an engine driver, used to drive these express trains up to Glasgow, so he was gone most nights.
And it would be just my sister and I in the house.
So that was what I was doing then.
Then afterwards I left school and I went to evening classes at a technical school.
And there would be bombs.
But people were very nice.
If there-- if there were an air raid and you'd be walking along a street, people used to drag you in the house and offer you a cup of tea, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] this kind of thing.
It was a wonderful spirit despite the danger, but a type of spirit that perhaps it only comes about when you have danger like that.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That was pretty much it.
I got a job then in the country, in Tring.
And I was traveling backwards and forwards weekends working there.
And I was there for quite a while.
Now, my guardian, Mr.
Griggs, he was about to retire-- well, the war's still going on.
But he retired after the war, just after the war.
And about three months after he retired, he died.
The other gentleman had lived in this same house for many, many years, also an engine driver.
He had also died.
And the two children of Mr.
Griggs, Charlie and Kitty, both came back to London, so there were four of us living in this house.
And we kind of knew that something had to happen, you know, we-- as far as living somewhere else.
We couldn't just continue like that.
At first, we of course thought that maybe they'll find our parents, we'll return to our parents, and life, you know, will go back to what it was.
But by the end of '45 or '46, the news of what actually took place in Europe became fully known, and we knew that we didn't have any family left there, no aunts, uncles, no parents, no one.
No one survived.
And it was really now up to-- up to us whether we wanted to continue to live as we were or try and pick up the pieces and try and maintain or become-- lead a Jewish life.
And probably more my sister than myself was the mover in that direction.
I guess I was quite happy with my friends and activities that went on.
But I think she was perhaps thinking about futures more seriously.
And we made-- she made contact through some friends of hers with a family from-- that brought us to Manchester.

And I think it was important to move away, because you can't have a group of friends and live one way one day one way, and then the next day say, you know, gee, I'm-- everything's changed, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because at that time, it seemed like there was only the orthodoxy and not being Jewish.
I didn't know any in-betweens.
So we moved to Manchester, and--
My sister and I, yes.
And we took lodgings in two different homes actually.
And some people helped us find jobs.
And then gradually I found my-- my own ways.
I found that the Habonim youth movement had-- it was a Zionist youth movement-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had a lot of the answers to things that I was looking for in life.
I found lots of friends there.

I also found that after having lived in England for five, six years or more and almost forgotten or not thinking of myself as a refugee anymore, all of a sudden I became one in the eyes of these people.
But in London, I wasn't looked upon like that.
That was-- that was hard at first.
But pretty soon I made lots of friends there in Manchester.
And I enjoyed-- very much enjoyed the life then.
I'm still in touch with some of the people from there.
And then the British government made a gesture to children such as we who had lost their parents.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they offered them British nationality without them having to go through the normal thing of publications or writing and applying and all that.
All you had to do was just say yes, sign a piece of paper, and that's it.
So we both did that and became British.
So then everything went along fine.
And when I was about 18 or before 18, 17 and 1/2, I got my call-up papers, so I went to have my medical and tests and all that sort of thing, and I passed that.
They said, OK, we'll notify you when-- when we want you.
Meanwhile, the situation in Israel, or Palestine then, was-- was changing.
The war had started.
And-- and of course, being-- especially being in the Zionist movement, I felt-- I felt the need to be there, rather than being in the British army.
So at some point I made the decision that that's what I want to do.
And I went to my youth movement first, because a lot of the youth movements were doing recruiting at the same time.
Well, my youth movement, they only wanted to send me to a kibbutz.
They didn't want me to join the army.
So I had to find another youth movement that would allow-- would get me through to the military line.
And I did that.
It was all kind of cloak and dagger stuff the way it worked then.

You went to a poke little office in London, where somebody would give you a ticket to board a boat train to France and write on the edge of a newspaper something in Hebrew, roll it in a ball, stick it in your pocket, and that was your identity to give to somebody else when you arrive in Paris.
And memorize this address, and I was walking up and down Oxford Street memorizing this address.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then-- and also rehearsing what to say if the-- if the British customs asks you, you know, how come you only got a one-way ticket and all that.
Well, you know, a lot of this was in my head.
When I went through the customs, they say where are you going?
So I said, I'm going on vacation.
Well, have a great time.
It didn't bother him one bit.
And once we got in Paris, in Marseilles, they already-- the porter there knew that we were going to Israel.
It was such a usual route.
And so that was what happened.
I went to Israel and joined the army there.
Meanwhile, the police was-- were coming to my house in Manchester-- I lived with my sister-- looking for me.
So the first time my sister said, well, he went on vacation.
He should have been back, but he's not back.
Second time, she gave some excuse.
The third time, she told them, well, we've got a card from him.
He's in Israel, so by that time it was known.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Well, I was there for about a year and a half.
And I was discharged.
I-- I went with a group of volunteers.
And this was just for a period of the war.
Once it was over, we were discharged and given tickets to go back home.
So in my case, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I decided to go back and report to the army.
And it was kind of a test.
I didn't know what was going to happen, whether they're going to put me in jail, because I was AWOL for quite a while.
And I went back.
They knew on my arrival-- they had documentation on me.
The police came to my door at home the next day.
They told me where I had to report and when.
And when I reported there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a note, see adjutant.
The adjutant is the guy who's in charge of all discipline in the regiment.
So everybody was saying, oh, boy.
You're in trouble.
You're in trouble.
What's even stranger is that this very regiment was one of the last regiments to leave Palestine.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was an armored regiment that was well known by the Haganah and the Jews around in Israel, or Palestine at that time, running around in their armored vehicles searching for arms and all that.
And they became a training regiment now.
So I saw the adjutant.
And he said to me, you know, we could throw the book at you.
You've been AWOL for a year and a half.
He said, but you're here now.
And-- and I think the main thing was that I didn't try and evade the army by running away from it.
The fact I joined another army for-- out of conscience had a certain respect for it.
And he said, but we're not going to take that into account.
You're here now.
You'll go-- you'll just do your service.
And then he started asking me about places there, whether the Haifa Refinery was still standing or whether it was-- he must have had some stock in that.
But--
So my service there was OK.
But you'd asked earlier about anti-Semitism.
Well, amongst the group, my intake, there wasn't any.
I really had a great bunch of friends amongst them.
But one night, my drill sergeant came around in the middle of night.
He'd been drinking heavily.
And he knocked on the door there, barrack room, and called me out.
And he was going to take my head off.
He was going to do all kinds of things.
And I tried to talk to him, reason him.
Well, he was telling me about some of his friends that were killed by Israelis, either with Stern group or [INAUDIBLE] or somebody.
I mean, he lost somebody, some of his friends over there.
And I guess I was a representative of those by his measure.
Anyway, he didn't do anything.
I went back in the barrack room and all of the guys were bunched around the door.
They said, well, if he would have touched you, we would have all been out there and all that.
It was good to have that support.
But I didn't report it to anybody.
But somebody had mentioned it to my troop sergeant.
And the next day he said, well, do you want to make a report on this?
So I said, no.
I don't.
I understand his feelings.
I'd sooner forget about the whole incident.
And he said, well, if it happens again, whether you want to or not, I'll report it, because we don't tolerate this in the British army.
And I thought, boy, that-- that really reflected well on the British army, the way they behaved.
And I never had any incidents.
And the rest of it, it never kind of followed me either.
I don't think it was anti-Semitism.
It was being on the other side.
It's a type of thing that somebody here felt about Jane-- what's her name?-- when-- when she went to North Korea, North Vietnam.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jane--
I can't think of her name.
But it's-- it's that kind of feeling.
Two years.
I went to merchant marine academy in England.
Before I left Israel, I knew I wanted to come back.
And I scouted around, what will I do there?
And I knew that I wanted to see the world.
I wanted to save some money so I could live somewhere and make friends.
And I put those three things together and I said, boy, the thing to do is join-- get trained, join the Israeli merchant navy, and I'll be able to do all of those things.
And so I went to see them at the office and asked if I went and trained, would I be able to get a job there?
And they said yes, we'll welcome you.
And so that's exactly what I did.
So from England, from the British army, you then went to the Israeli merchant marines.
Well, I went to the academy first.
And then I spent some time on English ships too in the meantime.
But then afterwards, I went on Israeli ships.
Was in '56 until '63.
No.
I don't-- I mean, everybody knew where was I from, that-- that sort of thing.
In-- in Israel, there are so many stories, so many horror stories that my story is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nothing extraordinary to talk about, so I never particularly talked about it there.
If anybody wanted to ask me anything, I could.
And in the States, it's exactly the opposite.
When-- people usually say where are you from, I detect an English accent, so I say I'm from England.

I don't usually go into the bit about, well, actually, I was born in Austria, I came there, because it opens up a whole lot of questions that I frankly don't particularly want to get into with just anybody that I'm acquainted with.
So-- so really, I find that I'm never really talking about these issues.
'56 to '63, that's seven years.
Came to the States.
I think it was '57.
She was a passenger on the ship.
And we met and-- and had a normal kind of attraction boy meets girl have.

But at the same time, her background and my background, I think in addition to having-- wanting to live in Israel as well, we had so many things in common that gave us a real kind of common interest that was beyond just the fascination of-- of physical attraction.
So we were seeing each other every trip I came here.
So it was kind of a long-distance courtship for a year or more.
And then we got married here.
And what made you decide to leave Israel?
Oh, that was-- you see, I thought I'd planned everything perfectly.
I had planned what kind of a job to take to do at sea.
I even took electronics and radio at sea so that I could move into the-- leave the ships and get a job ashore, which I did.
I worked for IBM there.
The only thing you can't plan is how healthy your kids are going to be.
And my son, older son who's autistic, we didn't know what-- actually what he had or what he was.
We just knew there was a problem.
He had lots of different labels.
They said he was aphasic.
And so we went and had therapy once a week.
But then that therapy wasn't-- couldn't be paid for anymore by the medical plan.
And the person who was giving the therapy was trained in America.
And I remember she told us that, you know, if you go to the States, there are special schools for this kind of problem and they'd be there six hours a day, seven hours a day every day.
And this is what you should do for him.
Well, because I was with IBM, I managed to get a transfer here.
Josie, having an American passport, she could just come back anytime anyway.
And-- and also, with my British passport, I had no problem coming here.
So for us, the move to the States, compared to people from other countries, was a real simple one.
I had a job.
I spoke the language.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was just a matter of picking up and coming.
Now, we thought that this might be for a couple years and some miraculous treatment is going to take place and we'll go back again.
Well, after we came, we found there were half a dozen more diagnoses and labels, and things really didn't work out like that.
And so finally, we had to sell our apartment there and realize that this is-- this is our permanent situation here.
Not that it's bad, but just not what I'd planned.
Well, America was not new to me, because I had been for four years on Israel-America line with-- on the ships.
So I had been backwards and forwards here many times.
I was quite familiar with the American scene, even though I hadn't contemplating living here.
So I-- I don't believe I had any tremendous impact, because it wasn't-- it wasn't totally new.
I'd been here too many times.
It was an easy adjustment.
I think the difficulty-- I found that the work experience here was very different to Israel.
In Israel, IBM was a new-- fairly new entity.
And all the people working for IBM were young people and I was young.
And you felt like you were in a new adventure.
Whatever you were doing, it had an impact on the country and you felt like you were in the forefront of something important doing something like that.
And then when I came here, they'd been around here for ages.
I suddenly saw guys in their 50s and 60s dragging tool bags around and cursing the day they ever joined the company and all that.
You know, this-- this was a culture shock to me.
I'd never seen that before.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So it was a great deal of adjustment.
And I realized that here, your workplace and your private lives are totally separated.
There, it was very much part of it.
Our friends were from my work.
And in fact, I still have friends from my work.
And when I go to Israel now and it's 30-odd years after I left there, we still correspond and we still meet up and meet each other.
And yet, it didn't-- this isn't the scene here, except in rare occasions.
People leave their work at work and don't usually socialize.
I may have mentioned a few things.
I mean, people recognize an accent, so they ask you where you're from and all that and-- or how you met your wife.
Those kind of things come up.
And I'm, you know, quite happy talking about that and experiences at sea.
But I never get into the-- what I call the heavy things, talk about my family, talk about those days that I find it's difficult to talk or speak about.
I kind of avoid-- avoid those subjects, because they're painful and because I'm not sure I can handle it.
So as a result, they don't get spoken of.
Oh, yeah.
Yes.
As a matter of fact, Charlie, the boy, who now has just retired, was here earlier this year.
He has a daughter living in Ohio, so he was visiting there, and then he spent a week here.
His sister, whenever we go to England, we see her.
She's been to Israel a couple of times.
And we correspond every few months, so we've kept in touch.
Personally, against me, no.
I mean, I've heard people say things.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I've heard people say things about just about every grouping that we have here.
But more than that I-- I can't say I've felt.
No.
I've never been back.
And a lot of people have asked me that.
At one time, I used to feel that the only way I want to return to Vienna is in a bomber.

But I think lately, you know, I've kind of given way to curiosity, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that maybe I wouldn't mind one day just looking at where we lived and-- but otherwise, I have no fond memories of people there to go to.
